terms.definition: A name that refers to (is bound to) an object in memory. In Python the name has no type of its own; the type lives on the object it points to.
The classification of a value that determines what it can hold and which operations are valid on it (e.g. int, str, list).
Types are associated with values at runtime, so a single variable can be rebound to objects of different types over time.
The language does not implicitly convert between unrelated types; mixing incompatible types (like adding a str to an int) raises an error instead of guessing.
An object whose contents can be changed in place after creation, such as list, dict and set.
An object that cannot be changed after creation; any "modification" produces a new object. Examples: int, float, str, bool, tuple.
The binding from a name to the object it points to. Assignment copies the reference, not the underlying object, so two names can share one mutable object.
Optional annotation syntax (PEP 484) declaring the expected type of a variable, parameter or return value. It is informational and not enforced by the interpreter at runtime.
A fixed value written directly in source code, e.g. 42, 0.7, "prompt" or [1, 2].
